eli3: Stacking is an ensemble method that uses a second model (the meta-learner) to combine the predictions of several base models. Think of it as hiring several specialists — a doctor, an economist, and a statistician — to each give you an opinion, then hiring an expert moderator to weigh and combine their opinions intelligently.

The key insight over simple averaging: instead of treating all models equally, the meta-learner learns which base models are reliable in which situations. If one model is great for certain input patterns and another is great for others, the meta-learner learns to weight them accordingly.

The trick that prevents overfitting: base model predictions are generated on held-out data (cross-validation), never on the data they were trained on. This ensures the meta-learner sees realistic predictions, not artificially good in-sample predictions.

whenWhy.title: Squeezing out the last 0.5–1% accuracy
whenWhy.body: Stacking consistently outperforms any single model and often beats simple averaging, especially when base models are diverse (low correlation). In Kaggle competitions, stacking ensembles are almost always present in top solutions. The gains are most significant when combining fundamentally different model types (tree-based + linear + neural).

whenWhy.title: Diverse base model combination
whenWhy.body: Stacking is most effective when base models make different errors — e.g. one tree-based model, one linear model, one neural network. The meta-learner learns to weight them based on their complementary strengths and can outperform each individual model.

whenWhy.title: Final layer in production ML pipelines
whenWhy.body: In production, a stacking ensemble can be used to combine predictions from multiple specialised models trained on different feature subsets or data views — giving robust predictions even when one model degrades (e.g. due to data drift).

whenWhy.title: When you have sufficient data
whenWhy.body: Stacking requires enough data to: (1) train base models on each fold, (2) generate OOF predictions for training the meta-learner, and (3) evaluate the final ensemble. With very small datasets, the overhead of cross-fitting reduces the training size for each base model, potentially hurting performance.

deepDiveIntro: Stacking (Wolpert, 1992) — also called Stacked Generalisation — trains a meta-learner on the out-of-fold (OOF) predictions of K base learners. The base learners are level-0 models; the meta-learner is the level-1 model.

The critical requirement is that the meta-learner must be trained on OOF predictions — predictions generated by each base model on data it was not trained on. This prevents the meta-learner from exploiting the artificially low training errors of the base models.

deepDive.heading: Out-of-Fold (OOF) Predictions
deepDive.body: Use K-fold cross-validation. For fold k: train each base model on the other K−1 folds, generate predictions on fold k. After all K folds, each training point xᵢ has one OOF prediction from each base model — as if it was never seen during training. These OOF predictions form the training data for the meta-learner. [1]
deepDive.formula: ẑ_ij = prediction of model j on xᵢ, trained on all folds except the one containing xᵢ

deepDive.heading: Meta-Learner Training
deepDive.body: The meta-feature matrix Z ∈ ℝⁿˣᴷ (rows = training examples, columns = base model OOF predictions) is the training input for the meta-learner. The meta-learner predicts y from Z. Common meta-learner choices: linear/logistic regression (interpretable, low variance), Ridge/Lasso, GBM, or another stacking layer. Use a simple, regularised meta-learner to avoid overfitting. [1]

deepDive.heading: Test Set Predictions
deepDive.body: For test data, each base model is typically retrained on the full training set and used to generate test predictions. Alternatively, average the K fold models. The meta-learner then takes these test predictions as features and produces the final prediction. This is a critical step that is sometimes done wrong — always retrain on the full training set for production. [1]

deepDive.heading: Why OOF is Required
deepDive.body: If the meta-learner is trained on in-sample base model predictions, the base models will appear nearly perfect (low training error), making the meta-learner learn to trust them unconditionally. When the same base models are applied to test data, their errors are much higher than the meta-learner expected, and the stacked model underperforms. OOF predictions have the same distributional properties as test predictions. [1]

deepDive.heading: Blending vs Stacking
deepDive.body: Blending is a simpler variant: split training data into a 'blend' set (e.g. 20%) and a 'train' set (80%). Train base models on the train set, generate predictions on the blend set, train the meta-learner on blend set predictions. Faster and easier to implement but uses less data for base model training. Stacking (full OOF) is more data-efficient and generally preferred. [1]

takeaways: Stacking trains a meta-learner on OOF predictions from K base models — the meta-learner learns how to best combine them. [1]
OOF predictions are critical: the meta-learner must never see in-sample predictions from base models. [1]
Diversity of base models is key — combine different algorithm families (trees, linear, neural) for maximum benefit. [1]
Use a simple, regularised meta-learner (logistic regression, Ridge, shallow GBM) to avoid overfitting at level 1. [1]
For test predictions, retrain each base model on the full training set — do not use the fold models directly. [1]
Blending (holdout-based) is a simpler alternative to full K-fold stacking — same idea, less data-efficient. [1]

edgeCases.q: How many base models do you need for stacking to help?
edgeCases.a: You need at least 2 diverse base models. In practice, 3–8 base models covering different algorithm families (GBM, Random Forest, linear model, neural network, k-NN) provide good diversity. Adding very similar models (e.g. XGBoost and LightGBM with similar hyperparameters) adds little diversity and can introduce redundancy. More models also increase the computational cost of OOF cross-validation. [1]

No — use a simple, regularised model. The meta-learner only has K features (one per base model), so it doesn't need high capacity. Logistic regression or Ridge regression is usually sufficient. A complex meta-learner (deep GBM) will overfit to the OOF predictions' noise. For Kaggle, shallow GBM or even a weighted average tuned by Nelder-Mead optimisation often outperforms a complex meta-learner. [1]

You can stack multiple levels: level-0 base models → level-1 meta-learner (itself an ensemble) → level-2 meta-meta-learner. Each level uses OOF predictions from the previous. In practice, 2 levels is almost always sufficient — additional levels rarely add meaningful accuracy and multiply computational cost. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does stacking differ from boosting?
edgeCases.a: Boosting trains models sequentially, each correcting the errors of the previous — models are not independent. Stacking trains base models independently in parallel, then learns how to combine them. Boosting reduces bias through sequential correction; stacking reduces both bias and variance by combining diverse parallel models. They are complementary and often combined (stack several GBM models with different hyperparameters). [1]

sources.label: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 8.8
